paragraphs:
1/49
Once Upon a Time in America:
the Mafia and the Unions
Giovanni Mastrobuoni
Collegio Carlo Alberto, UNITO, CEPR, IZA
Andrea Matranga
UNITO
Marta Troya Martinez
UAB, NES, BSE and CEPR
Big thanks to: Maxim Alekseev, Alexander Basov, Lucia Bessone, Simran Dhalla,
Angelika Gareeva, Kristina Goncharova, Alexandra Grigoryeva, Anastasia
Khromenko, Vladislav Lapatkin, Alexander Lutsenko, Anastasia Mityushkina,
Anastasia Nebolsina, Alessandro Perga, Sofia Rubstova, Kirill Ryazantsev, Andrea
Scalanghe, Sara Shahbazi, Daniil Starikov, Corinne Smith, Polina Uvarova and
Mikhail Zhokhov
Bolzano, 2025

2/49
Mafia and Unions in the US
“My family [Genovese] (...)
got money from
gambling, but our real power, our real strength, came
from the unions. With the unions behind us, we could
shut down the city, or the country for that matter.
— Vicent “Fish” Cafaro, Senate Permanent Subcom-
mittee on Investigations, 1988”

3/49
Mafia and Unions in the US
Many countries have both organized crime and organized
labor, but only in the US did the two develop a largely
symbiotic relationship
Long history of media attention, from Elia Kazan
Hollywood’s classic 1954 to Martin Scorsese’s 2019
Netflix movie...
−→

4/49
Research gap
... but almost no attention by labor law scholars or
economists (some work by historians and sociologists)
Labor racketeering [...] has undermined the labor
movement, one of the most powerful and important
sociopolitical institutions. While the problem has at-
tracted enormous law enforcement, political, and me-
dia attention [...] it has attracted practically no crim-
inological attention [...] or interest among labor law
scholars.” — Jacobs & Peters (2003) p.271
It is important to understand the consequences of this
muddy partnership, given:
the dramatic (and unexplained) decline in the
American labor movement - with its detrimental
consequences on inequality (Farber et al. 2021)
the recent resurgence of unions (Gallup, 2022)

5/49
The origins of mobbed-up unions
In late 19th century, the rise of the labor movement
triggered strikes & violence between workers and
employers =⇒employers recruited gangsters and workers
paid the mafia for protection ≈the emergence of Mafia in
Italy (Gambetta, 1996 & Acemoglu et. al., 2019)
With the end of Prohibition (1933), the Mafia settled into
this new business opportunity and barely faced any
opposition (or got rid of it):
FBI not interested in fighting organized crime =⇒
Hoover, the FBI Director, denied the existence of a
centralized and organized mafia until 1957
(Appalachian meeting) and refused to devote
investigative resources
“Communist” unions were a greater evil

6/49
Mafia uses of Infiltrated Unions
Corruption: paying themselves, family members and
associates from union funds
Politics: using the “legal” cover for organized crime
leaders to meet business owners and political figures
Economy: setting labor terms, setting, monitoring and
enforcing producer cartel agreements between companies,
restricting entry

7/49
Today
We use census, historical & declassified law-enforcement
datasets covering most of the 20th century
We show that unions prone to be mafia behaved
differently:
in Mafia counties, the local union leader was more likely to be
Italian and, their surnames tend to originate from Palermo
in “captured unions,” convicted union leaders are
over-represented, are orders of magnitude more likely to be
Italian, and to originate from Palermo
Italians workers have different labor outcomes:
when compared to other immigrant groups, Italians are more
likely to work in Mafia controlled areas and show larger wage
growth from 1950s onward.
Sicilians (especially from Palermo) do particularly well
(ongoing work)

8/49
Related Literature
On mafia:
Origins of the Mafia: Bandiera (2003), Buonanno et al (2013),
Acemoglu, De Feo and De Luca (2020)
Mafia as a firm: Collection in Fiorentini & Peltzman (1997),
Reuter (1983), Abadinsky (1990), Gambetta (1996), Kumar &
Skaperdas (2009), Campaniello, Gray & Mastrobuoni (2016)
Structure of the Mafia: Garoupa (2007), Baccara and
Bar-Isaac (2008), Mastrobuoni (2015)
Economic cost of the Mafia: Pinotti (2015), Dippopa
(2021), Le Moglie & Sorrenti (2022), Daniele & Dipoppa
(2022), Di Cataldo & Mastrorocco (2022), Fenizia & Saggio
(2024), Mastrobuoni et. al (2024) =⇒all in Italy and we have
a focus on labor outcomes
On unions:
and inequality: Callaway & Collins (2018), Collins & Niemesh
(2019), Farber, Herbst, Kuziemko & Naidu (2021)
and mafia: Jacobs & Peters (2003), Jacobs, Cunningham &
Friday (2004), Jacobs (2006), Venturini (2023) =⇒all in
sociology except Venturini that focuses on corruption

10/49
Mafia locations: FBN
Mafia locations from the Federal Bureau of Narcotics
We have the exact facsimile of a secret report
documenting in detail 800 mafia members up until 1960
These files come from more than 20 years of
investigations, and several successful infiltrations by
undercover agents (Mastrobuoni, 2015)
This knowledge did not translate immediately in any
prosecution:
“Harry Anslinger, Director of the FBN, had much more
interest in and knowledge of organized crime than Hoover, but
he did not have the resources or the jurisdiction to act on this
knowledge.” (Jacobs, 2006, p. 10)
641 reside in the US, the remaining 159 reside mostly in
Italy =⇒80 “mafia” counties (= 42 commuting zones)

11/49
Mafia locations: FBN
Example of a record of a labor racketeer (out of 42)

12/49
80 Mafia-prone counties
Use the crosswalk from Autor and Dorn (2013) to get 42 CZ

13/49
Mafia-prone industries
Some industries are more prone to labor racketeering
NY Organized Crime Task Force classifies mafia prone
industries as ranking high in: 1) racketeering susceptibility
(strategic importance of the union within the industry) and,
2) racketeering potential (potential revenue derived from the
union exploitation)
Several sources (Jacobs work, industry prevalence in RICO
cases and mobsters’ jobs) point at the following industries:
Construction
Trucking services, Warehousing and storage, Water
transportation
Eating and drinking places, Liquor stores, Meat products
Hotels and lodging places
Sanitary services
This classification is prone to misclassification, biasing the
differences with non-mafia prone industries towards 0

14/49
Union leader prosecutions
Digitized 2277 prosecutions against union leaders for
racketeering, bribery, extortion, embezzlement and other
manipulation of union operations for criminal gain:
1968-1978: from U.S. Department of Labor reports
1979-1984: from the Kaufman (1986) - 1983
President’s Commission on Organized Crime, it
contains prosecutions from more participating
agencies
For each prosecution, we have the indictment, the
conviction, the name of the union leader and the union he
or she belonged to

15/49
Local union leaders: Teamsters
Teamsters was the largest union in the US (peaked with
over 2.3 million members) — this happened while the
union was led by James Hoffa from the late 50s to the
early 70s
Teamsters represented a wide variety of industries such as
truck drivers and construction workers
Collected the (1903-2008) rosters for Teamsters locals
with leader name, industries and location

16/49
Italian origin
Attribute Italian origin to the leaders’ names using two
data sources:
1) frequency of Italian surnames registered in Ellis Island
(1892-1924) — was the busiest immigrant inspection
station in the US
2) Italy is the country with the highest incidence and/or
density for a name using https://forebears.io/
Example for Teamsters
More
Collected data from the registry of the 542,763 dead
Italian soldiers in the World War I
(https://www.cadutigrandeguerra.it) =⇒Soldiers
surname associated with an Italian province of birth

17/49
Labor outcomes
Wages and employment from the IPUMS census
(1940-1970)
We define Italian family as being born in Italy or having
an Italian-born father - from 1980 ancestry is self-reported
Sample restriction:
male
householders
in households with no more than 2 adults
aged 16-55

18/49
Labor outcomes II
Restricted IPUMS census (1860-1950)
We have names and last names of individuals of Italian
origin
We look at labor outcomes separately for last names that
tend to originate from Palermo, Sicily.

19/49
Results

20/49
Italian Immigration over Time
We use the surnames of the Italian dead WWI sol-
diers to attribute an Italian province of origin to the mafiosi
We look at the evolution of the stock.

29/49
Italian origin of the mafia members
Composition of the FBN mafiosi dataset and Italians at
the immigration port of entry of Ellis Islands.

30/49
Italian origin of the mafia members
with FBI’s Dead list from the Record/Information
Dissemination Section of the FBI (1985-2010)

30/49
Italian origin of the mafia members
with FBI’s Dead list from the Record/Information
Dissemination Section of the FBI (1985-2010)
⇒Italian-American mafia was mainly formed by people of
Italian origin. In large part from the Palermo province.

31/49
From Local to General...
“Mob-controlled locals elected mob-controlled officers, who
chose mob-controlled convention delegates, who ratified the
choice of mob-controlled international presidents, vice
presidents, and general executive board (GEB) members.
Because the rank and tile did not vote directly for
international officers, PCOC judged it “unlikey, . . . that a
reform-minded Teamster president can be elected in the near
future” (PCOC 1986, p. 104).

32/49
Control of local union leadership
“LCN achieved working control of a local union by placing one
of its members or an associate in the position of top union
official or by intimidating or recruiting a union leader to do its
bidding.” (Jacobs 2006, p. 31).
Are Italians more prevalent among the union leaders in
mafia locations?
Where are these Italians originating from?
Are leaders more likely to remain in power?
Are the Italian teamsters leaders more likely to be
“associated” with the Mafia?

33/49
Union infiltration: Teamsters
For Teamsters, we explore if in mafia locations union
locals look any differently
Teamsters was the largest union in the US (peaked with
over 2.3 million members) — this happened while the
union was led by James Hoffa from the late 50s to the
early 70s
It was also known to be infiltrated by mafia: Teamsters
“have been firmly under the influence of organized crime
since the 1950’s.”, President’s Commission on Organized
Crime, The Edge: OCBLU, 1986 p. 90”

34/49
Italian local union leaders
The local leadership of Teamster becomes more “Italian” as
the mafia becomes more powerful but only in Mafia counties.
Differences stop growing in the mid-70s.

35/49
Italian local union leaders
This increase in “Italian leadership” (×10 in 80 years) does
not seem to be caused by the growth in the Italian population
in those locations (×2 in 80 years)

36/49
Italian origin of the leaders
Origins of Italian Teamsters leaders in counties with and
without mafia presence.

37/49

38/49
Share of Mafia related news
Search Lexis Nexis “Teamsters local leader name” + “County of
the Union” + “Mafia or similar” and compare to placebo words,
i.e. “Club””

38/49
Share of Mafia related news
Search Lexis Nexis “Teamsters local leader name” + “County of
the Union” + “Mafia or similar” and compare to placebo words,
i.e. “Club””
⇒What about the other Unions?

39/49
Local union leaders’ prosecutions
Unions with the most documented history of labor
racketeering: International Brotherhood of Teamsters (IBT),
Hotel and Restaurant Employees International Union (HERE),
Laborers’ International Union of America (LIUNA), and
International Longshoremen’s Association (ILA), Edge (1986)

40/49
Local union leaders’ prosecutions

41/49
Local union leaders’ prosecutions

42/49
Provincial origins of indicted union leaders, in infiltrated
and non-infiltrated unions

43/49
Workers
After controlling the leadership, the “next step was to
consolidate control through a combination of sticks and
carrots. (...) If the dissident depended on the union for
job assignments through a hiring hall, as is the case in the
construction industry and in longshoring, he would find
himself unemployed.(...) mob figures who control a union
local have many “carrots” to distribute. (...) friends an
supporters get all sort of perks, including high salaries.,
Jacobs (2006)
Since ethnicity plays a key role in the leadership of mafia
influenced unions =⇒turn to explore the workers behavior
Italian workers are potentially “cooperative” workers =⇒
may have found the job through Italian networks; likely to
be better informed about mafia culture, etc.
We look at Italian workers in mafia and non-mafia
locations and in mafia and non-mafia prone industries =⇒
compare them to other Europeans

44/49
Employment in mafia-prone industries
Italians are more likely to be employed in mafia industries
compared to other Europeans (controlling for CZ FE &
socioeconomic characteristics)
This is specially true in mafia locations

45/49
Wage income
In 1940, Italians had the lowest wages
Within a decade, Italians saw an enormous wage gain,
particularly in mafia industries and mafia locations

46/49
Difference in wage income
In these mafia-prone jobs, wages increased by almost 40
percent within just 10 years
These wages stayed higher than any other group

47/49
Wage Growth Map Based on Surnames

48/49
Summary and future steps
We are the first to study the mafia infiltration into labor
unions
We show that mafia members are likely to be originary
from Sicily, hence validating the within Italy signal of
mafia presence
(Teamsters) Unions in those locations are more likely to
be lead by an Italian leader and remain as an incumbent
The unions from likely captured unions are more likely to
face prosecutions and those leaders are likely to be from
Italian origin
Starting in the 1940s Italians are more likely to work in
Mafia CZs and Mafia industries, experiencing the largest
increase in wages
Current task: use names of Italians in the CENSUS to see
whether the Sicilians were the “winners.”

49/49
Local union leaders: % Teamsters with Italian Surnames
Both measures are highly correlated
